title: Vietnamese Caramel Fish (Ca Kho To)

ingredients.name: firm fish fillets or steaks (tilapia, catfish, mackerel)

subs.name: 400 g firm tofu steaks, pressed and dried, for a vegan version
subs.notes: reduce braising time to 10 minutes; caramel will still coat beautifully

subs.name: jaggery or palm sugar for a deeper, molasses-like caramel
subs.notes: watch closely as jaggery burns faster than white sugar

subs.name: 3 tbsp soy sauce for a fish-free version
subs.notes: the caramel will still be richly flavoured

ingredients.name: spring onions, cut into 3 cm lengths

steps.text: Pat the fish pieces dry with paper towel — surface moisture prevents caramelisation. Season lightly with salt on both sides and set aside.

Make the caramel: place the sugar in a small heavy saucepan or clay pot over medium heat. Do not stir — just swirl the pan occasionally. Watch carefully as the sugar melts and turns from golden to a deep amber, about 4-5 minutes. The moment it is dark amber (not black), remove from heat briefly.

Carefully add 100 ml of hot water to the caramel — it will sputter and steam dramatically. Return to heat and stir until the caramel dissolves into the water, then add fish sauce, minced garlic, ginger matchsticks and black pepper. Stir to combine.

Heat oil in a wide frying pan or clay pot over medium-high heat. Place the fish pieces in a single layer and sear for 2 minutes per side without moving them, until lightly golden. This builds flavour and helps the fish hold together during braising.

Pour the caramel sauce over the seared fish. Bring to a simmer over medium heat. Cook uncovered for 15-20 minutes, spooning the sauce over the fish every 4-5 minutes, until the sauce reduces to a thick, dark, glossy glaze that coats the fish. Taste — it should be deeply savoury and sticky-sweet.

Scatter spring onion lengths and sliced green chilli over the top in the last 2 minutes of cooking. Serve hot directly from the pot with steamed white rice — the dark braising liquid is the best part; spoon it over the rice.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: fish → 400 g firm tofu steaks, pressed and dried, for a vegan version (reduce braising time to 10 minutes; caramel will still coat beautifully); sugar → jaggery or palm sugar for a deeper, molasses-like caramel (watch closely as jaggery burns faster than white sugar); fish-sauce → 3 tbsp soy sauce for a fish-free version (the caramel will still be richly flavoured)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A heavy-bottomed kadai or non-stick pan works as well as a clay pot. Any firm fish available in Indian markets is perfect — mackerel (bangda), tilapia, surmai (seer fish) or catfish all braise well. Caramel-making is the only technical step; use white sugar (cheeni) over medium gas flame. The dish is a natural fit for the Indian palate — the sweet-savoury combination is reminiscent of Parsi and Goan sweet-sour fish preparations.
